github.name: world-cup-2026-predictor
github.description: Calibrated ML system forecasting the 2026 FIFA World Cup from 150+ years of matches: leakage-free Elo/form features, time-decayed XGBoost + Poisson models, walk-forward backtesting, and a 50k-run Monte-Carlo tournament simulator.

github.description: Rust + Python Topological ML Toolkit for persistent homology, Betti-curve features, manifold embeddings, topology docs, backend contracts, and E2E benchmarks for sklearn/PyTorch/TensorFlow-style pipelines.

trends: Today's tech landscape is heavily shaped by the rapid advancement and deployment of **agentic AI**, moving beyond mere chat interfaces to systems capable of proactive reasoning, planning, and real-world interaction. This includes automating complex tasks and bridging digital AI with physical IoT devices. There's a strong parallel emphasis on **personalization and localization of AI**, enabling models to run on personal data and devices to enhance privacy and tailored experiences. Significant effort is also being directed towards establishing **robust benchmarks and universal taxonomies** to evaluate, standardize, and improve these advanced AI capabilities and ensure numerical stability in evolving models. Concurrently, **AI-powered automation tools** are flourishing, streamlining workflows from content generation across multiple platforms to internal development processes, directly enhancing overall productivity. Underpinning these technical shifts, there's a growing community focus on **developer well-being, sustainable productivity, continuous learning paths (e.g., '100 days of AI'), and the creation of seamless, 'invisible' tools** that minimize friction and combat burnout.

ideas.title: SmartAgent Personal Workflow Orchestrator
ideas.description: Develop an AI agent that observes and learns a user's common digital workflows across various applications (e.g., summarizing emails, drafting social media posts, organizing files, recurring data entry). The agent will then proactively suggest automations or even execute tasks on behalf of the user, aiming to make digital tools 'invisible' and seamless. It will prioritize local processing of personal data for enhanced privacy, only using external APIs for general knowledge or public-facing content generation. This project directly addresses the rise of agentic AI and personalized productivity, making AI a more integrated part of daily work.
tech_stack: Local LLM Inference Engine (e.g., Ollama, Llama.cpp for privacy)
Desktop Notification System (e.g., Electron for cross-platform GUI)
OAuth2/API integration for various web services (Gmail, Slack, Social Media)
ideas.source_inspiration: OmniPost-AI (AI-powered publishing automation), Homekit (AI agent physical control), texts-to-transformer (local data processing), Cognitive-Core-Skills (agent reasoning taxonomies), Good Tools Are Invisible (philosophy of seamless tools).
steps: Task 1: Design a modular agent architecture for observing user actions and integrating with different applications (e.g., browser, email client).
Task 2: Implement a local data capture and storage mechanism for user workflow patterns, ensuring data privacy and local storage.
Task 3: Integrate a local LLM for intent recognition, task decomposition, and content generation based on observed patterns.
Task 4: Develop a GUI (e.g., Electron-based desktop app) for user interaction, task suggestions, and approval workflows.
Task 5: Implement initial automations for 2-3 common user tasks (e.g., 'draft email response', 'summarize web article', 'post social media update').
Task 6: Add robust error handling and user feedback loops for continuous agent improvement and trust.

ideas.title: AI SkillForge & Benchmark Hub
ideas.description: Create a web platform designed to help developers and AI enthusiasts systematically learn and benchmark their understanding of AI concepts, particularly focusing on 'cognitive core skills' for LLMs and agents. Users can embark on learning paths (inspired by '100 Days of AI'), engage with conceptual explanations, and then test their comprehension or the performance of small AI models against community-contributed or predefined benchmarks for specific skills like 'reasoning', 'perception', or 'planning'. The platform would track individual progress and offer insights into areas needing improvement, fostering a structured approach to AI education and agent development.
tech_stack: Python (Django/FastAPI for backend API and task execution)
PostgreSQL (database for user data, learning paths, benchmark results)
Markdown/MDX (for content delivery of AI concepts)
Unit testing frameworks (e.g., Pytest) for benchmark validation
ideas.source_inspiration: Cognitive-Core-Skills (taxonomy for AI agent skills), 100-days-of-ai (structured learning challenge), UniClawBench (universal benchmark for agents).
steps: Task 1: Set up a web framework (e.g., Django) and database to manage users, learning modules, and benchmark definitions.
Task 2: Design and implement a core set of 'cognitive skill' categories and associated conceptual content, drawing from the Cognitive-Core-Skills taxonomy.
Task 3: Develop a mechanism for users to submit code snippets or prompts for evaluation against predefined benchmark tasks (e.g., simple reasoning puzzles, pattern recognition).
Task 4: Implement an execution environment (e.g., using Docker containers) to run user-submitted code/prompts safely and score them.
Task 5: Create a user dashboard to track progress through learning modules and display benchmark performance over time.
Task 6: Add features for community contribution of new skill definitions, learning content, and benchmark challenges.

ideas.title: ResearchLineage Pro: Local Semantic Knowledge Graph
ideas.description: Build a desktop application that empowers researchers and developers to create a local, private 'knowledge graph' from their personal documents (research papers, codebases, meeting notes, chat logs, email archives, even iMessage exports). Utilizing local embedding models and semantic search, the tool will automatically identify relationships and trace the 'lineage' of ideas across disparate sources, inspired by how scientific ideas evolve. Users can semantically query their entire knowledge base, discover implicit connections, and get suggestions for new research directions or problem-solving approaches based on their unique, private data, fostering a deeper understanding of their own intellectual ecosystem.
tech_stack: Python (main application logic, data ingestion, NLP)
Local Embedding Models (e.g., Sentence-BERT, instructor-xl run via Hugging Face Transformers/Ollama)
Vector Database (e.g., ChromaDB, LanceDB for local storage and retrieval)
Graph Database (e.g., Neo4j embedded, or custom graph representation for relationships)
PDF/Docx parsers (e.g., PyPDF2, python-docx) for document ingestion
ideas.source_inspiration: Ideas Have Genomes (scientific lineage reasoning), texts-to-transformer (personal data analysis), Good Tools Are Invisible (seamless integration), UniClawBench (agents processing real-world data).
steps: Task 1: Develop robust document ingestion modules to parse various file types (PDF, Markdown, .txt, code files, iMessage exports) and extract text.
Task 2: Implement a local embedding pipeline to convert ingested text into vector representations, ensuring all processing stays on the user's machine.
Task 3: Design and implement a local vector database for efficient semantic search across the personal knowledge base.
Task 4: Develop a knowledge graph component to identify and store relationships between ideas, concepts, and documents, forming 'lineages'.
Task 5: Create an intuitive desktop GUI for uploading/indexing documents, performing semantic queries, visualizing connections, and exploring idea lineages.
Task 6: Implement a 'suggestion engine' that leverages the knowledge graph to propose new connections or research avenues based on the user's existing content.

quick_actions: Action 1: Dedicate 15-30 minutes daily to experiment with a new AI agent framework (e.g., AutoGen, LangChain agents) or learn about LLM agent architectures, leveraging resources like 'Cognitive-Core-Skills' for skill understanding.

Action 2: Identify one small, repetitive digital task you perform regularly (e.g., organizing screenshots, drafting a specific type of email) and spend an hour this week building a simple script or configuring an existing tool to automate it, aiming for an 'invisible' solution.

Action 3: Schedule 15 minutes at the end of each workday for a 'mental health check-in' – step away from screens, engage in a quick non-work activity, or practice mindfulness to combat burnout, inspired by recent Dev.to discussions.

Action 4: Choose a core tool or language you frequently use but haven't deeply explored in a while (e.g., a specific database, a JavaScript framework). Revisit its official documentation for 30 minutes this week, focusing on advanced features or overlooked fundamental concepts.
